name: Time and Work

sourceRefs.label: tnpscguru.in - 18-question real past-paper set on Time and Work

researchNotes: This is the 10th and FINAL named Aptitude skill, completing all of Part B Unit I. 18 real past-paper questions found on one source, covering many distinct shapes — picked ~9 representative ones spanning men-days inverse proportion, multi-worker combinations, pipes (fill+drain), wages-by-work-share, and two classic 'trap' questions (the spider-web problem and the persons-projects-days problem, both designed to trick you into multiplying instead of recognizing the ratio stays constant).

reading.intro: Time and Work is the last of the 10 named Aptitude skills — after this, Part B moves to its Reasoning unit. Real exam evidence shows heavy use of 'trap' questions designed to look like they need complex math but actually resolve via a simple ratio.

qa.question: Method — inverse proportion (more workers, less time): 12 men complete a work in 36 days. In how many days will 18 men finish the same work?
qa.answer: Men × Days = constant (total work). 12×36 = 18×x → x = 432/18 = 24 days. More workers means proportionally fewer days.

qa.question: Method — sequential combined work: A, B, C can complete a job alone in 8, 12, 16 days respectively. A and C work together for 2 days, then C leaves and B joins A. In how many more days do A and B finish?
qa.answer: Work rates: A=1/8, B=1/12, C=1/16 per day. In 2 days, A+C complete 2×(1/8+1/16)=2×(3/16)=3/8 of the work. Remaining = 5/8. A+B's combined rate = 1/8+1/12 = 5/24 per day. Days needed = (5/8)÷(5/24) = (5/8)×(24/5) = 3 days.

qa.question: Method — wages split by share of work done: A can do a job in 10 days, B in 15 days. They work together for 5 days, then C finishes the remaining work in 2 days. Total payment for the whole job is Rs.5,000 — how should it be split?
qa.answer: Pay is split in proportion to how much of the TOTAL work each person actually did, not how many days they worked. In 5 days, A completes 5/10=1/2 of the job, B completes 5/15=1/3; together that's 5/6, leaving 1/6 for C to finish. Split of Rs.5,000 by work share: A gets 1/2×5000=Rs.2,500; B gets 1/3×5000≈Rs.1,667; C gets 1/6×5000≈Rs.833. The key insight is the split follows WORK DONE, not days worked — C worked fewer days than B but did a comparable or different share depending on the rates, and pay tracks output, not time.

qa.question: Method — pairwise combined rates → solve for all three together: A+B finish a job in 12 days, B+C in 15 days, C+A in 20 days. In how many days can A, B, C finish it together?
qa.answer: Add all three pairwise rates: (A+B)+(B+C)+(C+A) = 1/12+1/15+1/20 = 2(A+B+C)'s rate. LCM=60: 5/60+4/60+3/60=12/60=1/5. So 2×(A+B+C rate)=1/5 → (A+B+C) rate=1/10 → together they finish in 10 days.

qa.question: Trap question: If 9 spiders make 9 webs in 9 days, how many days does 1 spider take to make 1 web?
qa.answer: 9 days — NOT 1 day. Each spider makes 1 web in 9 days regardless of how many spiders work in parallel (they're not collaborating on the SAME web). This tests whether you recognize the rate-per-individual stays fixed, the classic trap being to assume the answer scales down to 1.

qa.question: Method — pipes filling and draining together: Two pipes fill a tank in 30 and 40 minutes separately; a third pipe drains it in 24 minutes. With all three open, how long to fill the tank?
qa.answer: Combined rate = 1/30+1/40−1/24 (subtract the draining pipe's rate). LCM=120: 4/120+3/120−5/120 = 2/120 = 1/60. So the tank fills in 60 minutes.

qa.question: Method — provisions lasting fewer days with more people (inverse proportion again): 1,000 soldiers have 70 days of provisions. If 400 more soldiers join, how long will provisions last?
qa.answer: People × Days = constant (total provisions). 1000×70 = 1400×x → x = 70000/1400 = 50 days.

qa.question: Method — find the second worker's solo time from a combined time and the first worker's solo time: A and B together finish a job in 16 days; A alone takes 48 days. How long does B alone take?
qa.answer: B's rate = (A+B)'s rate − A's rate = 1/16 − 1/48 = 3/48−1/48 = 2/48 = 1/24. So B alone takes 24 days.

qa.question: Trap question: If 5 persons complete 5 projects in 5 days, how long do 50 persons take to complete 50 projects?
qa.answer: Still 5 days — NOT 50 days. The ratio of people to projects stays the same (1:1), so the per-project time doesn't change; 50 people working on 50 projects is just 10 independent copies of the original 5-person/5-project scenario happening in parallel.

questions.prompt: 15 men complete a work in 24 days. In how many days will 20 men finish the same work?
A and B together can finish a job in 18 days; A alone takes 45 days. How long does B alone take?
If 7 workers build 7 walls in 7 days, how many days does 1 worker take to build 1 wall?
questions.explanation: 7 days — each worker's individual rate is unaffected by how many work in parallel on separate walls.

questions.prompt: Two pipes fill a tank in 12 and 15 minutes separately; a third pipe drains it in 20 minutes. With all three open, how long to fill the tank?
800 soldiers have provisions for 60 days. If 200 more soldiers join, how long will the provisions last?
A, B, and C can do a job alone in 24, 30, and 40 days respectively. Working together, how long will they take?
If 8 people complete 8 tasks in 8 days, how long do 40 people take to complete 40 tasks?
questions.explanation: Still 8 days — the people-to-task ratio (1:1) is unchanged, so per-task time is unchanged.
